tldr: Move transactional sending from SendGrid to Resend for a cleaner DX, first-class React Email support, and a simpler API surface. SMTP fallback exists on both sides; most apps need only a credential + from-domain swap. Re-warm any dedicated IPs and re-import suppressions before cutover.
why_switch: Developer experience: terser SDKs, modern docs, fewer footguns than SendGrid's v3 API.
First-class React Email integration — author templates as JSX components instead of Handlebars/Dynamic Templates.
Simpler API surface: one resource (emails) instead of multiple overlapping mail/marketing endpoints.
Cleaner domain + DKIM setup flow; faster onboarding for a single sender domain.
Open-source ecosystem (React Email is OSS) reduces template lock-in.
Webhook + event model focused on transactional needs, less marketing-bloat.
Pricing parity at the entry tier (~$20/mo on both for ~50K emails) with no high-water-mark on Resend's hybrid plan.
what_to_export: Sending domains and their DKIM/SPF/DMARC records (re-verify on Resend).
Suppression / bounce / spam / unsubscribe lists (export via SendGrid Suppressions API).
Dynamic Templates and Legacy Templates (HTML + Handlebars source).
Marketing contacts and lists, if any (note: limited Resend equivalent — see gotchas).
Automations / scheduled sends (likely require manual reimplementation).
Event webhooks configuration (URL, signed key, subscribed events).
API keys and their scopes (rotate, do not copy values — generate new on Resend).
IP pool assignments and dedicated IPs (Resend dedicated IP availability/cost differs).
Subusers / teammates and role mappings (re-create in Resend org).
Inbound parse / reply-handling routes (verify Resend equivalent before cutover).
Click/open tracking settings and link-branding domain.
Sender identities (verified single senders) and from-name conventions.
export_method: Use SendGrid REST API + dashboard exports: Suppressions API for bounces/blocks/spam/unsubscribes (CSV), Templates API for template HTML, Marketing Contacts export (CSV) from the UI, Webhooks settings page for event URLs, Domain Authentication page for DNS records. Keep API keys list for rotation tracking. No bulk one-click export — script per-resource pulls.
import_method: On Resend: add and verify each sending domain (DKIM/SPF), then create API keys and webhooks. Re-upload suppressions via the Resend API (or honor them in app logic before sending). Port templates by either keeping HTML strings or rewriting in React Email. Update SDK calls or SMTP credentials in app code. Run a shadow-send window where both providers are wired but only Resend sends to a small % of traffic; ramp up over days while monitoring bounce/complaint rates.

concept_mapping.resend: React Email components or raw HTML
Dedicated IP (availability and pricing differ — confirm)
Click/open tracking + custom return-path / tracking domain

effort: 1–3 weeks for a typical app. Same-day if it's a single transactional flow with one domain. Longer if you have many templates, dedicated IPs needing re-warm, or marketing automations to rebuild.
gotchas: IP warm-up: a new dedicated IP needs gradual ramp; cutting full volume on day one risks deliverability dips.
Dedicated IP availability and cost on Resend differ from SendGrid — confirm pricing before assuming parity.
Suppression list must be re-imported (or enforced in your app) before sending, or you'll re-mail addresses that previously bounced/unsubscribed.
Deliverability reputation is per-domain and per-IP — expect a short adjustment window even on shared IPs.
Marketing features (campaigns, automations, signup forms) are weaker on Resend; if you rely on these, plan a separate tool or stay on SendGrid for that slice.
Handlebars templates don't port 1:1 — either keep them as HTML strings or rewrite in React Email; substitution syntax differs.
Webhook signature schemes are different — update verification code, don't reuse SendGrid's signing logic.
Inbound Parse / reply handling: confirm Resend's inbound capability matches your routes before cutover.

email.subject: Migrating our transactional email from SendGrid to Resend
email.body:
Hey team,

I'm planning a move from SendGrid to Resend for transactional email. Quick rationale:

- Cleaner DX and simpler API (one /emails endpoint).
- React Email support — templates as JSX, less Handlebars glue.
- Pricing parity at our tier; no obvious lock-in.

Plan:
1. Add and verify our sending domains on Resend (DKIM/SPF).
2. Export suppressions and templates from SendGrid; re-import / rewrite on Resend.
3. Wire Resend behind a feature flag, shadow-send a small % of traffic, monitor bounce/complaint rates.
4. Ramp to 100% over ~1 week; keep SendGrid creds live as a rollback for another week.

Open questions: do we need a dedicated IP on Resend (cost + warm-up), and does Resend cover our inbound-parse and any marketing flows? I'll confirm before cutover.

Timeline estimate: 1–3 weeks end to end. I'll share the rollout doc once domains are verified.

Thanks
